title: Choucroute garnie
subtitle: Alsatian braised sauerkraut with smoked sausage and pork belly
excerpt: Sauerkraut braised slowly in Riesling with onions, juniper berries, caraway, and bay, layered with pork belly and smoked sausage. An Alsatian dish that is as much a process as a recipe: three hours of gentle braising turns sour cabbage into something complex and deeply savoury.
sourceNotes: Choucroute garnie is the defining dish of Alsatian cuisine, a region on the border of France and Germany with a Germanic culinary tradition. It appears in both French and German cookbooks from the nineteenth century. The Alsatian version uses Riesling from the local vineyards rather than beer, which distinguishes it from the Bavarian and Polish variants.

content.text: Choucroute garnie means garnished sauerkraut, which undersells the result. The sauerkraut braises in wine and aromatics for two and a half hours until it is soft, silky, and has absorbed enough fat and flavour from the pork to become something entirely different from the sharp, raw product you start with. The sausages go in for the last thirty minutes, long enough to heat through and release their smoke into the dish without drying out.

Use jarred or vacuum-packed sauerkraut and rinse it well before cooking: the fermentation tang needs to be mellowed, not amplified. If you want more sharpness at the end, reserve a small amount of the unrinsed kraut and stir it in just before serving.

items.name: Pork belly
items.prepNote: cut into thick lardons or left as a piece

content.text: Brown the pork and sweat the onions

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the {{pork-belly}} on all sides, about 5 minutes. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}} and {{garlic}} to the fat in the pan and cook over medium heat for 10 minutes until soft and golden.

Add the {{sauerkraut}}, {{juniper-berries}}, {{caraway-seeds}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{thyme}} to the casserole and stir to combine with the onion. Nestle the pork belly pieces among the sauerkraut. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of wine and {{stock-chicken}} of stock. The liquid should come about halfway up the sauerkraut. Bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven. Braise for 2 hours.

After 2 hours, nestle the {{smoked-pork-sausage}} into the sauerkraut, cover, and return to the oven for 30 minutes more. Remove the bay leaves and thyme. Taste and season with pepper, the pork and sausage will provide the salt. Serve in the casserole or on a large platter with boiled waxy potatoes and Dijon mustard on the side.

items.fix: Rinse and squeeze sauerkraut very thoroughly; the long braise mellow the acidity. It needs the full 2 hours
items.cause: Not rinsed well enough before cooking, or braised for insufficient time
items.symptom: Sauerkraut is still sharp and acidic after braising

items.fix: Always add smoked sausages in the last 30 minutes; they are already cooked and only need warming through
items.cause: Added too early and cooked for the full 2.5 hours
items.symptom: Sausages have dried out and split

items.fix: Add a splash of stock or water halfway through and check the liquid level; the sauerkraut should be moist but not swimming
items.cause: Liquid evaporated under a tight lid in a hot oven

items.fix: 1 teaspoon is plenty; lightly crush the seeds before adding to release fragrance without bitterness
items.cause: Too much seed, or seeds not crushed

content.text: Alsace sits on the Rhine between France and Germany, and its cuisine reflects both: French wine culture and German pork culture, in a region that has changed hands four times since 1870. Choucroute garnie is the dish that most clearly shows this dual inheritance, French Riesling, German sauerkraut and smoked sausage technique, cooked together for hours in a way that makes the ingredients inseparable. In Strasbourg, it appears in brasseries year-round, though it is primarily a cold-months dish. The versions served in Paris brasseries tend to be more elaborate, but the Alsatian original is simpler and better.
